commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a doctor auth-profile repair that rewrites canonical-store `api_key` fields to `key`, wires it into doctor health, and adds focused regression coverage.

PR surface: Source +112, Tests +151. Total +263 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main ignores `api_key` while eligibility and resolution only consume `key`/`keyRef`; I did not run a live current-main command in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: The remaining blocker is contributor real behavior proof or maintainer verification plus completed checks, not an automated code repair.

target.title: fix(doctor): rewrite non-canonical api_key field to key in auth profiles
target.body:
## Summary

Per the doctor-rewrite follow-up direction in #86163: runtime auth/config parsing should stay strict and read the canonical schema only, while tolerance for legacy/malformed files belongs in `openclaw doctor --fix`.

`auth-profiles.json` entries written with the snake_case `api_key` field (instead of the canonical `key`) are silently skipped at runtime, producing the misleading `No API key found for provider` error (Fixes #57389). The runtime parser deliberately stays strict.

This adds a doctor repair (`maybeRepairCanonicalApiKeyFieldAlias`) that:
- detects canonical-store profiles (`{ profiles: { ... } }`) whose `api_key` field is set but the canonical `key` is missing,
- explains that the canonical field is `key` and that runtime parsing ignores `api_key`,
- on `openclaw doctor --fix`, rewrites `api_key` to `key` and removes the non-canonical field, writing a timestamped backup first.

It reuses the existing candidate enumeration, backup, and note infrastructure in `doctor-auth-flat-profiles.ts`, and is wired into `runAuthProfileHealth` alongside the existing flat/oauth auth repairs. The runtime parser (`persisted.ts`) is left untouched, so invalid configuration is never silently accepted at runtime.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: a canonical `auth-profiles.json` profile written with the non-canonical `api_key` field is detected and rewritten to the canonical `key` by `openclaw doctor --fix`, with a backup, instead of being silently skipped at runtime.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head. Exercised the real `maybeRepairCanonicalApiKeyFieldAlias` doctor repair under the repo's vitest runner against a real on-disk `auth-profiles.json` written to a temporary state dir, with no mocking of the module under test.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: wrote a canonical `auth-profiles.json` containing a profile with the snake_case `api_key` field to a temp agent state dir, ran the doctor repair via the regression suite, then inspected the rewritten file and the backup on disk.

Evidence after fix: stdout from the vitest run against the real repair entry point, comparing pre-fix and post-fix:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-auth-canonical-api-key-alias.test.ts

-- BEFORE the fix (core rewrite disabled): the regression test fails --
x rewrites the non-canonical api_key field to key with a backup (57389)
  AssertionError: expected the on-disk profile to deeply equal the canonical shape.
  Expected the rewritten profile to contain   key: "sk-snake-case-key"
  Received the on-disk profile still containing   api_key: "sk-snake-case-key" (no key field)
  Tests 1 failed | 2 passed (3)

-- AFTER the fix (rewrite enabled): the regression test passes --
ok rewrites the non-canonical api_key field to key with a backup (57389)
ok does not touch profiles that already have the canonical key field
ok reports the non-canonical api_key field without rewriting when repair is declined
  Tests 3 passed (3)
```

Observed result after fix: before the fix the on-disk profile keeps the non-canonical `api_key` field with no `key`, so the runtime parser would still skip it; after the fix doctor rewrites the file so the profile becomes `{ type: "api_key", provider: "my-provider", key: "sk-snake-case-key" }` (other fields and the `order` map preserved untouched), and a timestamped `.api-key-alias.<ts>.bak` backup of the original is written. The doctor note output shows the detect/explain text and the rewrite/backup confirmation. The full suite (3 tests) passes.

What was not tested: no real provider credentials or network calls were used; the key value is a placeholder string. The change is confined to the doctor repair path; the runtime parser is intentionally not modified, per the #86163 direction that runtime parsing stays strict.
